chunks.text: Course: EE 248C | EECS at UC Berkeley EE 248C. Numerical Modeling and Analysis: Nonlinear Systems and Noise Catalog Description: Numerical modelling and analysis techniques are widely used in scientific and engineering practice; they are also an excellent vehicle for understanding and concretizing theory. This course covers topics important for a proper understanding of nonlinearity and noise: periodic steady state and envelope ("RF") analyses; oscillatory systems; nonstationary and phase noise; and homotopy/continuation techniques for solving "difficult" equation systems. An underlying theme of the course is relevance to different physical domains, from electronics (e.g., analog/RF/mixed-signal circuits, high-speed digital circuits, interconnect, etc.) to optics, nanotechnology, chemistry, biology and mechanics. Hands-on coding using the MATLAB-based Berkeley Model Units: 4 Also Offered As: ELENG 248C Course Objectives: Using MAPP for fast/convenient modelling and analysis RF (nonlinear periodic steady state) analysis - harmonic balance and shooting - Multi-time PDE and envelope methods - perturbation analysis of periodic systems (Floquet theory) Modelling and analysis of oscillatory systems - harmonic, ring and relaxation oscillators - oscillator steady state analysis - perturbation analysis of amplitude-stable oscillators RF (nonlinear, nonstationary) noise concepts and their application - cyclostationary noise analysis - concepts of phase noise in oscillators Homotopy techniques for robust nonlinear equation solution Student Learning Outcomes: Students will develop a facility in the above topics and be able to apply them widely across science and engineering. Prerequisites: Consent of Instructor Formats: Spring: 4.0 hours of lecture per week Grading Basis: Student Option Final Exam Status: No Links: EECS Course
